agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order lifecycle management for a system where orders progress through placement, invoicing, and shipping. Three stakeholder groups have provided conflicting requirements about when cancellation is permitted.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, progressing through a lifecycle from placement to shipment."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, preventing it from being fulfilled and shipped."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document generated to lock in pricing and record the obligation. Described by Finance as irreversible once created."},{"term":"Placement","definition":"The initial creation/confirmation of an order. Operations states invoicing occurs at this moment."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The act of dispatching goods to the customer. The PM defines this as the cancellation cutoff."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed and immediately cancelled","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — PM says allowed (not yet shipped), Finance says forbidden (already invoiced at placement per Ops)."},{"scenario":"Order placed, time passes, not yet shipped, customer requests cancellation","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — same contradiction applies. Invoice exists from placement, blocking cancellation per Finance, but PM permits it."},{"scenario":"Order placed but invoicing fails or is delayed","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — if Ops policy is intent ('we invoice at placement') but invoicing can technically fail, does a window exist? Spec does not address."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH — If cancellation is blocked at placement, refund/cancellation flows are effectively eliminated. If cancellation is allowed despite invoicing, Finance's revenue recognition and audit trail assumptions break. Getting this wrong has direct P&L and compliance implications.","operational":"HIGH — Warehouse, fulfillment, and shipping teams need a clear, unambiguous cancellation cutoff to avoid picking/packing orders that are later voided. Contradictory rules will cause process chaos.","user":"HIGH — Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment. If the system silently prevents all cancellations (due to immediate invoicing), customer satisfaction and support volume will be severely impacted.","risk":"CRITICAL — Implementing any single interpretation without stakeholder alignment risks violating either financial controls (Finance), customer commitments (PM), or operational procedures (Ops). Legal/compliance risk if invoicing rules are regulatory.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders if Finance rule prevails; poor experience, increased support contacts, potential chargeback increases."},{"group":"Finance / Accounting","impact":"Revenue recognition, audit trail, and pricing integrity are at stake. If cancellation is allowed post-invoice, they need a credit note / reversal process that contradicts 'irreversible.'"},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Core feature requirement (pre-ship cancellation) is impossible under current Finance + Ops constraints."},{"group":"Operations / Warehouse","impact":"Need deterministic cancellation rules to manage pick/pack workflows. Ambiguity causes wasted labor and inventory mismanagement."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the brunt of any policy confusion — need clear, defensible rules to communicate to customers."}]},"confidence":{"level":30,"reasoning":"Three authoritative stakeholder groups have issued requirements that form a logical contradiction. The PM's rule (cancel before ship) and Finance's rule (no cancel after invoice) are individually coherent but become mutually exclusive when combined with Ops' rule (invoice at placement). This is not an ambiguity — it is a hard contradiction. No acceptance criteria can be defined until stakeholders resolve the conflict. Confidence is very low because ANY implementation would violate at least one stakeholder's stated requirement.","high_confidence_areas":["The three statements, taken together, are logically contradictory — this is provable, not subjective","The domain terms (order, invoice, shipment, cancellation) are well-understood","The contradiction has material business impact across all stakeholder groups"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance means 'invoicing' as a financial document or a broader concept like 'billing intent'","Whether Ops truly invoices at the instant of placement or shortly after (async)","Whether 'irreversible' means the invoice record itself is immutable or that the financial commitment cannot be undone (credit notes may be acceptable)","Whether the PM requirement was written with awareness of the invoicing policy"],"assumptions":["All three statements are intended as hard requirements, not aspirational","'At the time of placement' means synchronously, not 'sometime after placement'","'Irreversible' means no credit notes, reversals, or voiding mechanisms exist"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements form a logical impossibility: (1) PM says cancel is allowed before shipment. (2) Finance says cancel is forbidden after invoicing. (3) Ops says invoicing happens at placement. Therefore: cancel is forbidden immediately at placement, which means cancel is NEVER allowed — directly contradicting the PM's requirement. At least one of these three statements must be relaxed or reinterpreted.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance","Operations","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["A: Relax Finance's rule — introduce a 'credit note' or 'invoice reversal' mechanism so that invoiced orders CAN be cancelled. Invoicing remains irreversible as a record, but a compensating transaction (credit memo) enables cancellation. This is standard practice in most ERP systems.","B: Relax Ops' timing — delay invoicing until a later stage (e.g., at pick/pack or at shipment). Orders can be cancelled freely before invoicing. This sacrifices price-lock-at-placement.","C: Relax PM's rule — accept that orders cannot be cancelled once placed. Inform customers before placement that orders are final. This sacrifices customer flexibility.","D: Introduce a 'pro-forma invoice' concept — Ops issues a non-binding price-lock document at placement (pro-forma), and the binding invoice is generated later (e.g., at shipment). This preserves price-locking without triggering Finance's irreversibility constraint."],"recommendation":"Option A or D. Option A (credit notes) is industry-standard in logistics and e-commerce — invoices are immutable records but credit memos are the standard compensating mechanism. Option D (pro-forma invoice) cleanly separates price-locking from financial commitment. Recommend a meeting between PM, Finance, and Ops to choose. Do NOT proceed with implementation until resolved."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Logical contradiction between three stakeholder requirements makes any implementation incorrect by at least one stakeholder's definition.","business_rule_broken":"PM's cancellation window, Finance's invoice immutability, and Ops' invoice-at-placement timing are mutually exclusive. Implementing any two violates the third."}]}
